topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed on my screen, stark white against the muted backdrop of my evening. My breath hitched, a sudden, shallow intake of air that settled cold in my chest. It was a listing. My childhood address. A house I hadn't thought about in years, now available. My fingers hovered over the screen, trembling slightly. I clicked, the link opening to a professional photo of the familiar front porch, eerily vacant. A knot tightened in my stomach, a slow, cold clenching that made my jaw ache. I immediately minimized the window, my gaze sweeping the darkened room as if expecting someone to materialize from the shadows. I started pacing the length of the living room, my footsteps muffled by the thick rug, repeating the same circuit, my eyes darting toward the front window every few seconds.

[story 2]

I stared at the listing for nearly an hour, my coffee growing cold in the mug. Every detail felt too precise: the overgrown rose bushes in the picture, the specific shade of blue on the shutters. A low hum started in my ears, a persistent, irritating frequency that made the hairs on my arms stand up. I checked the street beforehand, a nervous habit I couldn't shake. The neighborhood felt unnervingly quiet tonight, the usual sounds of evening traffic absent. I pulled my phone away, the glass cool against my palm. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool pane, trying to see past the reflection into the dark yard. A sudden, sharp intake of breath betrayed my unease, and I quickly turned away, forcing myself to focus on the mundane task of locking the door twice.

[story 3]

The email arrived at 3 AM. It was brief, professional, and devastatingly familiar. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat demanding attention. I sat bolt upright in bed, the sheets tangled around my legs. I didn't read the full description immediately; instead, I focused on the metadata, the agent's contact information. My hands shook as I typed a reply—a simple inquiry about the property's condition. Every keystroke felt heavy, deliberate, weighted with unspoken apprehension. When I hit send, I leaned back against the headboard, a slow, controlled exhale escaping me. I stayed there, perfectly still, listening to the silence of the apartment, waiting for a response that felt both inevitable and terrifyingly uncertain.
